./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:59:| D5 | **Wire transport**: GraphQL only. | All DDx↔Axon traffic is GraphQL — queries and mutations over HTTP, subscriptions over WebSocket. No gRPC, no REST, no MCP from the DDx side. The Go client is generated by `genqlient` from a pinned schema-introspection snapshot. Rationale: Axon's GraphQL surface already covers entity CRUD, links, traversal, transactions, schema, and subscriptions; DDx is already a GraphQL server internally (`cli/internal/server/graphql_*.go`), so DDx↔Axon is wire-symmetric; subscriptions unlock live UI updates for `ddx work` (Story 8) without polling; no protoc/codegen toolchain enters the DDx build. The per-call wire-overhead advantage of gRPC is immeasurable at DDx scale (low thousands of beads); if batch-transaction throughput later becomes painful for one specific path, gRPC can be added there without revisiting this decision. |

The per-call wire-overhead advantage of gRPC is immeasurable at DDx scale (low thousands of beads); if batch-transaction throughput later becomes painful for one specific path, gRPC can be added there without revisiting this decision. |

./docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:196:**Integration tests: zero mocks.** No exceptions. Integration tests exercise real production implementations of every dependency in the graph — real git (`t.TempDir()` + scrubbed `GIT_*` env), real filesystem, real bead store, real coordinator, real server, real agent harness (the `script` harness below is the only approved fake for the AI-provider boundary, and it does not mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically). A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.
./docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:209:**The only approved fake in the DDx test suite is the `script` harness.** It is the *AI-provider* boundary replacement, and it is not a mock — it is a deterministic alternate implementation of the `agent.Runner` interface that reads a line-based directive file and performs real filesystem + git operations against the worktree. The `virtual` harness is a replay-only fake retained for prompt-routing unit tests; it does not write files or make commits.
./docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:237:- `TestConcurrentWorkers*` tests that instantiate one mock git per goroutine → the shared-writer invariant is not being tested. Replace with real git + the `script` harness.

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:52:{"id":"corpus-368f5152b39b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"layer-2 design: should execute-bead workers create beads in-band?","description":"During investigation of ddx-7eab13a6 (bead-id resolver worktree bug), a design question surfaced: is it correct for execute-bead workers to call `ddx bead create` directly in-band?\n\nThree alternatives:\n\n(a) Append to parent bead. Worker appends discovered sub-tasks as structured items in the parent's notes or a new discovered-subtask event. The parent becomes the durable record; future runs or the operator can decompose.\n\n(b) Surface via result. Agent emits a structured discovered_subtasks: [...] field in result.json; the execute-loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.\n\n(c) Create beads in-band (current behavior). Worker calls ddx bead create directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue on over-decomposition.\n\nToday's behavior is (c) but undocumented. One worker spawned 11 read-coverage children in a single run (ddx-44236615), all P0, without operator review. The work may be valid but the pattern needs a documented position.\n\nAdditionally, 22 existing beads have malformed IDs of the form .execute-bead-wt-\u003cparent\u003e-\u003ctimestamp\u003e-\u003crandom\u003e-\u003chex\u003e (caused by the layer-1 bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6). A mass-repair or close+refile decision should be made once the design position is set.\n\nScope: pick (a), (b), or (c) and document in a spec amendment (FEAT-006 or FEAT-010 if applicable); record the rationale. Also decide whether to repair the 22 existing malformed-ID beads.","labels":["area:bead","area:agent","kind:design"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-d3a4f89c","title":"layer-2 design: should execute-bead workers create beads in-band?","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"During investigation of ddx-7eab13a6 (bead-id resolver worktree bug), a design question surfaced: is it correct for execute-bead workers to call `ddx bead create` directly in-band?\n\nThree alternatives:\n\n(a) Append to parent bead. Worker appends discovered sub-tasks as structured items in the parent's notes or a new discovered-subtask event. The parent becomes the durable record; future runs or the operator can decompose.\n\n(b) Surface via result. Agent emits a structured discovered_subtasks: [...] field in result.json; the execute-loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.\n\n(c) Create beads in-band (current behavior). Worker calls ddx bead create directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue on over-decomposition.\n\nToday's behavior is (c) but undocumented. One worker spawned 11 read-coverage children in a single run (ddx-44236615), all P0, without operator review. The work may be valid but the pattern needs a documented position.\n\nAdditionally, 22 existing beads have malformed IDs of the form .execute-bead-wt-\u003cparent\u003e-\u003ctimestamp\u003e-\u003crandom\u003e-\u003chex\u003e (caused by the layer-1 bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6). A mass-repair or close+refile decision should be made once the design position is set.\n\nScope: pick (a), (b), or (c) and document in a spec amendment (FEAT-006 or FEAT-010 if applicable); record the rationale. Also decide whether to repair the 22 existing malformed-ID beads.","labels":["area:bead","area:agent","kind:design"]}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:63:{"id":"corpus-42801b4c2e9b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"agent: hardcoded 15-minute DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout kills thinking-route-target requests; not configurable","description":"Source: cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:30\n  const DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute\n\nThis is hardcoded, not surfaced through .ddx/config.yaml or any flag, and is applied uniformly to every upstream-service regardless of route-target class. It is set on every service path:\n  - cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go:116  Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout\n  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:153           Upstream-serviceTimeout: DefaultUpstream-serviceRequestTimeout\n\nThe comment justifies it as 'Defeats RC4 of ddx-0a651925: a stalled TCP socket that has delivered headers but stopped emitting body bytes would otherwise pin a goroutine until the outer wall-clock (3h) frees it.'\n\nBut that scenario is already handled by a separate idle-read timeout immediately below, on line 34:\n  const DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute\n\nThe 5-minute idle-read timeout is exactly the right tool for the stalled-TCP-socket case (no body bytes for 5 min → fail). The 15-minute wall-clock cap on a single request is the WRONG tool — it kills any legitimately long single Chat call regardless of streaming health.\n\nConcrete impact observed today (2026-04-29 axon repo):\n\n1. axon-db7a6d0a worker (qwen3.6-35b-a3b on bragi via lmstudio)\n   - status: no_changes\n   - duration_ms: 1,762,081 (29 min total — one timeout + retry)\n   - log: 'WARN upstream-service error, retrying attempt=1 err=\"upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s\" delay=1s'\n   - Retry consumed another full 15 min, also timed out, no commits produced.\n\n2. axon-80979cb8 + axon-db7a6d0a + axon-ab2e52e0 (3-worker cascade re-launch)\n   - All 3 hit 'upstream-service request timeout: wall-clock 15m0s' on the first attempt\n   - User killed the run before retries could waste another 15 min × 3 each\n\nWhy thinking route-targets hit this:\nqwen3.6-35b-a3b and similar 'thinking' / chain-of-thought reasoning route-targets can spend 5–10+ minutes generating reasoning tokens before producing the first body delta the runner considers progress. Streaming output IS happening (so the idle-read timeout would NOT fire), but the runner is counting wall-clock against the entire request. A single hard problem can blow the 15-minute cap legitimately.\n\nWhy arbitrary timeouts are not helping:\n\na) Same value for cloud upstream-services (claude, codex) and local upstream-services (lmstudio, omlx). Local thinking route-targets routinely need \u003e15 min on hard prompts; claude rarely does. Same cap is wrong for both classes.\n\nb) No way to opt out of the cap per upstream-service, per route-target, per request, per project. /home/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/config.yaml has no knob.\n\nc) Retries inherit the same cap. A timeout-prone session retries with the same fate. Burns cycles and tokens.\n\nd) The cap defeats the cascade: when a cheap-tier qwen times out, escalation to standard claude is intended — but on a thinking route-target the timeout doesn't mean 'broken upstream-service', it means 'this prompt is hard'. We then escalate after wasting 15 min, and the standard tier solves the problem in 2. Net effect of cheap-tier-first becomes negative.","acceptance":"AC1. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-project via .ddx/config.yaml under agent.routing or agent.endpoints — e.g. agent.endpoints[upstream-service].request_timeout_seconds — and the current 15-minute hardcoded constant becomes a default that can be overridden upward (or removed entirely) per upstream-service.\n\nAC2. Upstream-serviceTimeout is configurable per-tier via the route-target-overrides path: cheap tier on a known thinking route-target (qwen3.6, deepseek-r1, etc.) defaults to a longer cap (e.g. 60 min or unlimited). Cloud chat route-targets keep the 15-min default.\n\nAC3. The idle-read timeout (DefaultUpstream-serviceIdleReadTimeout = 5 min) is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense; documentation in cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go and the user-facing config docs explain the relationship between idle-read and wall-clock-request timeouts and when each fires.\n\nAC4. When Upstream-serviceTimeout fires, the error message names the exact timeout setting and the path to override it (e.g. 'request_timeout=15m exceeded; configure agent.endpoints.\u003cname\u003e.request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml').\n\nAC5. execute-loop / execute-bead --request-timeout DURATION CLI flag exists for one-off overrides during debugging.\n\nAC6. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:66:{"id":"corpus-47f5902056ab","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Add project-scoped route middleware and migrate document/bead/graph routes","description":"## Scope\nAdd project-scoping middleware to the DDx server and migrate all document, bead, and doc-graph HTTP routes to canonical project-scoped paths.\n\n## Context\nThe server currently registers ~50 routes under /api/... without project scoping. FEAT-002 requires /api/projects/{project}/... as canonical. Three worker routes already use this pattern (handleProjectWorkerList, handleProjectWorkerShow, handleProjectWorkerProgress) — follow that pattern.\n\n## Implementation approach\n1. Create a projectMiddleware (or helper function) that:\n   - Extracts {project} from the URL path\n   - Resolves it via GetProjectByID() or GetProjectByPath()\n   - Injects the resolved ProjectEntry into request context\n   - Returns 404 if project not found\n2. Register all document, bead, and doc-graph routes under /api/projects/{project}/...\n3. Keep legacy /api/... routes as singleton compatibility aliases that auto-resolve to the single registered project (error if \u003e1 project registered without scoping)\n4. Add test(s) that register two projects, verify scoped routes resolve correctly, and verify legacy routes work with one project\n\n## Files to touch\n- cli/internal/server/server.go — route registration, middleware\n- cli/internal/server/handlers.go (or individual handler files) — extract project from context instead of hardcoding\n- cli/internal/server/server_test.go — new project-scoped routing tests\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n- MCP tools (separate bead)\n- GraphQL schema (already has beadsByProject, workersByProject)\n- Frontend routes","acceptance":"(1) A projectMiddleware or projectResolver extracts {project} from /api/projects/{project}/... routes and injects resolved ProjectEntry into request context. (2) Document routes (list, read, write, search), bead routes (list, show, create, update, claim, unclaim, reopen, ready, blocked, status, deps), and doc graph routes (graph, stale, deps, dependents, history, diff, show, write) are all registered under /api/projects/{project}/... in addition to existing legacy routes. (3) Legacy unscoped routes continue to work when exactly one project is registered (singleton compatibility). (4) cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/server -run 'TestProject' -count=1 passes with at least one test covering scoped route resolution.","labels":["phase:build","area:api","kind:implementation"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-36d2775f","title":"Add project-scoped route middleware and migrate document/bead/graph routes","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"## Scope\nAdd project-scoping middleware to the DDx server and migrate all document, bead, and doc-graph HTTP routes to canonical project-scoped paths.\n\n## Context\nThe server currently registers ~50 routes under /api/... without project scoping. Add test(s) that register two projects, verify scoped routes resolve correctly, and verify legacy routes work with one project\n\n## Files to touch\n- cli/internal/server/server.go — route registration, middleware\n- cli/internal/server/handlers.go (or individual handler files) — extract project from context instead of hardcoding\n- cli/internal/server/server_test.go — new project-scoped routing tests\n\n## Files NOT to touch\n- MCP tools (separate bead)\n- GraphQL schema (already has beadsByProject, workersByProject)\n- Frontend routes","acceptance":"(1) A projectMiddleware or projectResolver extracts {project} from /api/projects/{project}/... routes and injects resolved ProjectEntry into request context. (2) Document routes (list, read, write, search), bead routes (list, show, create, update, claim, unclaim, reopen, ready, blocked, status, deps), and doc graph routes (graph, stale, deps, dependents, history, diff, show, write) are all registered under /api/projects/{project}/... in addition to existing legacy routes. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:108:{"id":"corpus-720b519f6e95","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"correctness: move semantic failures out of route feedback","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1313 and docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1371\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: high\\nDescription: CONTRACT-003 says DDx reports semantic task outcomes through RecordRouteAttempt and that route-target-quality/task-failure contributes to route-target/runner reliability for future ranking. The clarified boundary is that agent does not track semantic success/failure directly. Agent only reports availability/network/protocol-level failure. Semantic task outcomes belong to the caller and may be contributed to the route-target catalog or catalog-derived power rating, not to agent's transient route feedback store.\\nSuggested fix: Remove route-target-quality/task-failure from RouteAttempt route-health semantics, redefine RecordRouteAttempt or a separate catalog-feedback mechanism for caller-provided semantic evidence, and document how that evidence affects catalog power ratings outside the agent dispatch loop.","acceptance":"CONTRACT-003 no longer states that semantic task failures directly demote route candidates in agent; docs distinguish upstream-service availability feedback from caller-owned semantic catalog feedback; failure classes exclude route-target-quality/task-failure from agent route-health penalties","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"bead":{"id":"agent-8460e36e","title":"correctness: move semantic failures out of route feedback","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1313 and docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md:1371\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: high\\nDescription: CONTRACT-003 says DDx reports semantic task outcomes through RecordRouteAttempt and that route-target-quality/task-failure contributes to route-target/runner reliability for future ranking. The clarified boundary is that agent does not track semantic success/failure directly. Agent only reports availability/network/protocol-level failure. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:117:{"id":"corpus-7c1aedcf73aa","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"execute-loop: push_failed should NOT set a 1-year cooldown — race-with-remote is recoverable","description":"Today, when execute-bead lands locally but push fails (e.g. non-fast-forward because remote advanced), the loop sets execute-loop-retry-after to ONE YEAR in the future. This is wrong on multiple axes:\n\n1. Push race is fundamentally recoverable: pull/merge/push is the standard fix and takes seconds.\n2. A 1-year cooldown means the bead never re-runs in any reasonable working horizon.\n3. The work is already done locally — the bead is functionally complete; reopening it for a year is misleading.\n\nObserved in this session on ddx-98e6e9ef (routing-preflight-gate): worker landed commit 978131c6, push rejected because origin/main had advanced (687fb4ca + 4a6c6e8a pushed remotely between the local land and the push). Loop set retry_after=2027-04-29T03:43:59Z. Operator had to manually merge, push, and clear the cooldown.\n\nWhat is needed:\n\n1. push_failed should auto-pull/merge/retry-push at least once before giving up. The merge case where origin advanced with non-overlapping work is the common case in a multi-worker / multi-operator setup.\n\n2. If the auto-merge has conflicts that the loop cannot resolve, push_failed should set a SHORT cooldown (5-15 min), not 1 year. The operator just needs time to resolve the conflict.\n\n3. push_failed-with-conflicts should mark the bead as needing-human-review (a structured outcome similar to declined_needs_decomposition from ddx-fba752b9), not as a generic execution failure.\n\n4. The loop's cooldown duration should NEVER exceed some reasonable cap (e.g. 24h) for any outcome. Year-scale cooldowns mean 'never' and that should be a deliberate operator choice via 'ddx bead update --set execute-loop-retry-after=...', not an automatic loop decision.","acceptance":"1. push_failed flow attempts auto pull --rebase / merge / retry-push at least once before exiting with push_failed. 2. push_failed cooldown caps at 24h regardless of underlying error. 3. push_failed-with-conflicts (auto-merge fails) emits a structured outcome distinct from generic execution_failed; bead is parked for human review with the conflict context recorded as a structured event (kind:push-conflict). 4. Regression test: a fake git that returns non-fast-forward on first push, then fast-forward after pull/merge, exercises the new flow and asserts the bead lands cleanly without operator intervention. 5. Regression test: a fake git that produces an unresolvable merge conflict exercises the structured-outcome path and asserts cooldown \u003c= 24h. 6. CHANGELOG entry. 7. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:139:{"id":"corpus-9725c274b8e3","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.\n\n## Evidence\n\nDDx-side session log:\n/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)\nParent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.\n\n## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)\n\nCommit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:\n1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.\n2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).\n\nThe workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.\n\n## Requested upstream fix\n\n- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller\n- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals\n- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort\n- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping\n\n## Scope\n\n- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)\n- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar\n- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern\n\n## Relationship to DDx\n\nWhen this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.","acceptance":"(1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.","labels":["agent","kind:bug","area:compaction","severity:high","upstream-request"],"bead":{"id":"agent-e5228424","title":"bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).\n\nThe workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `\u003ca\u003e` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:206:{"id":"corpus-e0489fc7c76e","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"test(service): tests calling agent.New() leak user config — break verify against base revisions","description":"Several service_*_test.go tests call agent.New(...) with default options. agent.New defaults to loading the user's \\$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/agent/config.yaml. Any upstream-service type added to the user's config that the agent module under test doesn't recognize causes those tests to fail with 'unknown upstream-service type'.\n\nToday's beadbench preflight run (agent-beadbench-preflight against agent-openrouter-qwen36plus, 2026-04-28) hit this: the bead executes correctly through the route-target loop, but the verify gate runs 'go test ./...' against the bead's verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (pre-lucebox). That older code rejects 'type: lucebox' / 'type: vllm' from the user's live config.\n\nFailing tests captured (sample):\n\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:680 TestNewWaitsBrieflyForInvalidQuotaRefresh\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:721 TestNewStartupQuotaRefreshContinuesAfterTimeout\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:765 TestPrimaryQuotaRefreshWorkerRefreshesOnTimer\n  service_validation_test.go:15 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessRoute-targetBeforeRun\n  service_validation_test.go:43 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessReasoningBeforeRun\n  service_runner_dispatch_test.go:41,90,119 TestExecute_Dispatches*\n  service_profiles_test.go:13,61,112 TestServiceProfiles_*\n\nAll same root cause: agent.New() loads the live user config without test isolation.\n\n## Fix paths\n\n1. Tests that don't actually need the user's config should pass an explicit ServiceConfig stub (no upstream-services, or a known shape).\n2. Or, set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a temp dir in test setup so agent.New() loads an empty config.\n3. Or, add an option to agent.New that skips XDG lookup.\n\nPick whichever is most consistent with how other test-isolation patterns work in this repo.\n\n## Why P2\n\nDoesn't block normal CI (CI clean container has no user config). Does affect any verify-gate run on a base revision predating a upstream-service-type addition — a bead correctness signal we care about during parity measurements.","acceptance":"1. service_*_test.go tests calling agent.New() with default options either set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a t.TempDir() or pass an explicit ServiceConfig that doesn't depend on the user's live config.\n2. 'go test ./...' from a verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (or any commit predating lucebox/vllm) passes regardless of what the runtime user has in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml.","labels":["area:test","area:service","area:config","kind:isolation"],"bead":{"id":"agent-27806ad5","title":"test(service): tests calling agent.New() leak user config — break verify against base revisions","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"Several service_*_test.go tests call agent.New(...) with default options. agent.New defaults to loading the user's \\$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/agent/config.yaml. That older code rejects 'type: lucebox' / 'type: vllm' from the user's live config.\n\nFailing tests captured (sample):\n\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:680 TestNewWaitsBrieflyForInvalidQuotaRefresh\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:721 TestNewStartupQuotaRefreshContinuesAfterTimeout\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:765 TestPrimaryQuotaRefreshWorkerRefreshesOnTimer\n  service_validation_test.go:15 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessRoute-targetBeforeRun\n  service_validation_test.go:43 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessReasoningBeforeRun\n  service_runner_dispatch_test.go:41,90,119 TestExecute_Dispatches*\n  service_profiles_test.go:13,61,112 TestServiceProfiles_*\n\nAll same root cause: agent.New() loads the live user config without test isolation.\n\n## Fix paths\n\n1. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:211:{"id":"corpus-e3d55d44b044","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead","description":"There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Runner, WorkerRecord.Route-target, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.\n\nThis bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.\n\n## Required changes\n\n### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)\nRegister alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:\n  --json         emit raw JSON array\n  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C\n  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)\n\n### 2. Data sources (two paths)\nA. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.\nB. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:\n   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)\n   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, runner, route-target, started_at\n\n### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan\nFor each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:\n  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)\n  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)\n  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available\n  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields\n\n### 4. Table output (default)\nColumns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | RUNNER | ROUTE-TARGET | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS\n- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)\n- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)\n- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID\n\n### 5. Merge and deduplicate\nWhen both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.\n\n## Key files\n- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()\n- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)\n- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","acceptance":"ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, RUNNER, ROUTE-TARGET, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.","labels":["ux","execute-loop","observability","cli"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-9060c8b2","title":"Add ddx agent workers command — native live worker status with current bead","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"There is no CLI command to see which workers are running and which bead each is executing. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.\nB. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:\n   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)\n   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, runner, route-target, started_at\n\n### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan\nFor each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:\n  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)\n  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)\n  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available\n  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields\n\n### 4. Table output (default)\nColumns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | RUNNER | ROUTE-TARGET | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS\n- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)\n- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)\n- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID\n\n### 5. Merge and deduplicate\nWhen both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.\n\n## Key files\n- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()\n- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)\n- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","acceptance":"ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, RUNNER, ROUTE-TARGET, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. Server-submitted workers show state from WorkerManager. Local --local workers are detected via manifest scan of .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories. --json flag emits raw JSON. --watch re-renders every 2s. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. No dependency on worktrunk or any external binary beyond git.","labels":["ux","execute-loop","observability","cli"]}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:229:{"id":"corpus-f546b016a732","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"Implement deterministic script runner for orchestration stress testing","description":"The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every \"fake\" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. Neither produces actual commits that the orchestration layer can rebase, merge, or land.\n\nThe consequence: every test that claims to cover concurrency, serialization, or git orchestration uses mocked git and therefore cannot catch merge-race, rebase-conflict, or coordinator-serialization bugs. `TestConcurrentWorkersNoMergeRace` at execute_bead_orchestrator_test.go:228 is particularly misleading — it uses separate orchTestGitOps instances per worker so the shared-writer invariant is never exercised. The test passes because no shared state exists to race on.\n\n## What this bead builds\n\nA new deterministic `script` runner (cli/internal/agent/script.go, mirroring cli/internal/agent/virtual.go):\n\n- Registered alongside claude/codex/agent/virtual in cli/internal/agent/registry.go\n- Always available (no binary probe required)\n- Takes a directive file (passed via RunOptions.Route-target or a new RunOptions.ScriptFile field — either works)\n- Reads the directive file, executes each line against opts.WorkDir as real filesystem + git operations\n- Returns a real Result{ExitCode, Output} that the loop and orchestrator can observe\n\n## Directive grammar\n\nLine-based, comments start with `#`. Each directive performs a real side effect:\n\n```\nappend-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003ctext\u003e          # append text to file (create if missing)\ncreate-file \u003cpath\u003e \u003ccontent\u003e       # create or overwrite file\ndelete-file \u003cpath\u003e                 # rm -f path\nmodify-line \u003cpath\u003e \u003cregex\u003e \u003crepl\u003e  # sed-style in-place substitution\nrun \u003cshell-command\u003e                # exec in WorkDir with clean env\ncommit \u003cmessage\u003e                   # git add -A \u0026\u0026 git commit -m \u003cmessage\u003e\nset-exit \u003ccode\u003e                    # final exit code (default 0)\nno-op                              # no commits, no files, exit 0\nfail-during \u003cindex\u003e                # synthetic failure at the Nth directive\nsleep-ms \u003cn\u003e                       # deterministic delay (for scheduling tests)\n```\n\nDirectives run in order. Environment variables DDX_BEAD_ID, DDX_ATTEMPT_ID, DDX_WORKER_ID are interpolated into text arguments so directives can be parameterized. Example:\n\n```\nappend-line tests/smoke.txt \"hello from ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\ncommit \"smoke: ${DDX_BEAD_ID}\"\n```\n\n## Invocation\n\nFrom the CLI: `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=./testdata/append-smoke.txt --prompt=/dev/null`\n\nProgrammatically from a test:\n```go\nrunner := agent.NewRunner(agent.Config{Runner: \"script\"})\nresult, err := runner.Run(agent.RunOptions{\n    WorkDir: wtPath,\n    Route-target:   \"testdata/append-smoke.txt\",\n    Correlation: map[string]string{\"bead_id\": beadID, \"attempt_id\": attemptID},\n})\n```\n\n## Implementation notes\n\n- Mirror the dispatch pattern in cli/internal/agent/runner.go:81 where `virtual` is intercepted\n- Always-available in Discover() same as virtual + agent\n- No external dependencies beyond what the agent package already uses (os, os/exec for git)\n- Estimated size: ~200 lines of implementation + ~200 lines of tests\n- Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go must prove each directive actually performs its claimed side effect on a real temp git repo\n\n## What this enables\n\nEvery test case in beads B (integration tests) and C (server runWorker regression test) depends on this runner existing. Without it, the land-coordinator redesign ships behind whatever real claude happens to do on the day the test runs.","acceptance":"New file cli/internal/agent/script.go implements RunScript(opts) that reads a directive file, executes directives against opts.WorkDir using real filesystem and git operations, and returns a real Result. The runner is registered in cli/internal/agent/registry.go as `script` and marked always-available in Discover(). Directive grammar supports at minimum: append-line, create-file, delete-file, commit, set-exit, no-op, sleep-ms, fail-during. Environment variable interpolation supports ${DDX_BEAD_ID}, ${DDX_ATTEMPT_ID}, ${DDX_WORKER_ID}. Unit tests in cli/internal/agent/script_test.go cover: (1) append-line + commit produces a real commit with the expected content, (2) no-op produces zero commits and exit 0, (3) create-file + commit produces the file and a commit, (4) set-exit 1 makes the run return exit 1, (5) fail-during 2 executes directives 0 and 1 but errors on directive 2, (6) env var interpolation replaces ${DDX_BEAD_ID} with the value from Correlation, (7) invoking via `ddx agent run --runner=script --route-target=\u003cfile\u003e` works end-to-end with a real temp git repo. go test ./internal/agent -run TestScript completes in under 5 seconds.","labels":["area:agent","area:testing","kind:infrastructure","phase:build"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-025046ac","title":"Implement deterministic script runner for orchestration stress testing","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"The execute-loop / execute-bead / land-coordinator system cannot be tested end-to-end today because every \"fake\" agent in the codebase returns a canned Result{} without actually writing files or making commits. `cli/internal/agent/virtual.go` is a prompt-response dictionary replayer — it returns canned stdout based on prompt hash but does not touch the filesystem or git. `fakeAgentRunner` and `fakeExecuteBeadGit` in `cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead_test.go` are in-memory mocks that track expected call counts. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:231:{"id":"corpus-f744aa57e91b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nObserved while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nTighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.\n\n## Scope\n- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn\n- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default\n- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state\n- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary","acceptance":"Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:correctness","area:agent","area:exec","area:git","area:cli"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-55f05e07","title":"Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nObserved while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nTighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.\n\n## Scope\n- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn\n- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default\n- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state\n- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary","acceptance":"Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:correctness","area:agent","area:exec","area:git","area:cli"],"parent":"ddx-32b3008e"}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:238:{"id":"corpus-011259e83fe4","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/\u003cid\u003e/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.\n\nVerified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.\n\n```\n$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json\n{\n  \"id\": \"worker-20260422T205846-f23f\",\n  \"kind\": \"execute-loop\",\n  \"state\": \"running\",\n  \"project_root\": \"/Users/erik/Projects/ddx\",\n  ...\n}\n```\n\nSo the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:\n\n```go\nif projectID != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.\n- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.\n\nThese are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.\n\nThe global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `\"\"` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.\n\nNote: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nTwo options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.\n\n### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site\n\n```go\nexpectedPath := \"\"\nif projectID != \"\" {\n    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)\n    if !ok {\n        return nil // unknown project id → no workers\n    }\n    expectedPath = proj.Path\n}\n...\nif expectedPath != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record\n\nAdd `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).\n\nRecommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.\n\n## Scope note\n\nThis is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).\n- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.","acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.\n\n2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**\n   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).\n   - Start one worker for each project.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-1\u003e)` returns exactly worker 1.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-2\u003e)` returns exactly worker 2.\n   - `workers` (global) returns both.\n   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.\n\n3. **Regression test — backward compat.**\n   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.\n\n4. **End-to-end validation.**\n   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.\n   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.\n\n5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**\n   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.\n   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.\n   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.\n\n6. **Cross-reference.**\n   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","bug","graphql"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-05b4cc9d","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.\n\nThis is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. ./docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md:480:Two-layer routing — DDx routing across harnesses (claude/codex/opencode/agent/pi), agent routing across providers (bragi/vidar/openrouter) — is **a long-standing requirement, badly implemented today**. Before locking the interface, do an archeology pass to find every existing spec, code piece, bead, and design note that touches it. The "repair" must be complete, not greenfield.
./docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md:486:**B. Lift all routing to DDx.** ddx-agent becomes a mechanism (list/probe/resolve/execute). DDx ranks `(harness, provider?, model)` tuples uniformly. Single-layer routing in DDx.
./docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-18-ddx-agent-service-interface.md:490:**Decisive C-favoring argument: comparison suite.** A stated project goal is that ddx-agent's native harness should *outperform* the subprocess wrappers (claude/codex/opencode) on batch noninteractive tasks. Proving that requires a comparison suite that runs identical prompts through every harness implementation and measures latency, cost, success rate, output quality. Today that suite is split: harness invocation lives in DDx (`cli/internal/agent/compare.go`, `cli/internal/agent/benchmark.go`), routing lives in DDx, but the native agent runtime lives in ddx-agent. Under C, the comparison suite collapses into one module that can A/B test (`harness=claude` subprocess) vs (`harness=agent-native`, `provider=bragi`, `model=qwen3.6`) with the same input/output schema and metrics collection. Under A or B, the suite still has to bridge two modules and measurement parity is harder to maintain.

./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:9:# API/Interface Contract: Epic Worker Surfaces
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:13:Define the server API, worker model, and UI surfaces for epic-scoped execution.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:14:Epic workers differ from single-ticket workers: they own a persistent branch
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:19:it. The two worker modes coexist on the same server.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:21:## Epic Worker Model
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:23:An epic worker is a long-lived worker that:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:46:- **DONE**: Epic merged or preserved, worker exits
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:57:- The epic worker may be configured to stop on first child failure or continue
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:61:After all children are processed, the epic worker evaluates a merge gate:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:72:- Worker ID and timestamp
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:76:### Worker Endpoints
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:78:All worker endpoints are shared between single-ticket and epic workers. The
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:79:worker record includes a `mode` field to distinguish them.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:81:#### `GET /api/agent/workers`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:83:List all workers. Supports filtering:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:87:| `mode` | `single`, `epic` | Filter by worker mode |
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:91:Response includes `mode` field on each worker record.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:93:#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:95:Show worker details. Epic workers include additional fields:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:112:Single-ticket workers include `"mode": "single"` and omit the epic fields.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:114:#### `POST /api/agent/workers/execute-epic`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:116:Start an epic worker. Request body:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:131:#### `POST /api/agent/workers/:id/stop`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:133:Gracefully stop a running worker. For epic workers, stops after the current
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:153:#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id/children`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:155:For epic workers, list child execution results:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:178:#### `GET /api/agent/workers/:id/log`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:180:Same as single-ticket worker log, but also includes child-level progress:
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:183:▶ epic worker started (model: z-ai/glm-5.1)
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:210:### `ddx server workers list --mode epic`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:212:Filter worker list to epic workers only.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:214:### `ddx server workers show <id>`
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:216:Works for both modes. Epic workers show child progress.
./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:218:## Worker Record Schema

./docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-002-epic-worker-surfaces.md:240:    WorkerModeEpic   WorkerMode = "epic"

./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-030-axon-bead-backend.md:59:| D5 | **Wire transport**: GraphQL only. | All DDx↔Axon traffic is GraphQL — queries and mutations over HTTP, subscriptions over WebSocket. No gRPC, no REST, no MCP from the DDx side. The Go client is generated by `genqlient` from a pinned schema-introspection snapshot. Rationale: Axon's GraphQL surface already covers entity CRUD, links, traversal, transactions, schema, and subscriptions; DDx is already a GraphQL server internally (`cli/internal/server/graphql_*.go`), so DDx↔Axon is wire-symmetric; subscriptions unlock live UI updates for `ddx work` (Story 8) without polling; no protoc/codegen toolchain enters the DDx build. The per-call wire-overhead advantage of gRPC is immeasurable at DDx scale (low thousands of beads); if batch-transaction throughput later becomes painful for one specific path, gRPC can be added there without revisiting this decision. |

./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:413:## 10. Worker-State Enumeration
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:415:Worker state is the in-process state of the drain loop. It is **distinct
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:417:for the worker's lifetime.
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:419:| Worker state | Meaning | Entry | Exit |
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:421:| `idle` | Worker is up but not actively claiming. | Worker startup; transient between attempts. | Operator or scheduler moves it to `draining`. |
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:422:| `draining` | Worker is actively claiming and executing beads. | Default operating state. | Quota or rate-limit pause; explicit stop. |
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:423:| `paused-quota` | Worker has observed quota exhaustion on its harness. | `drain-paused-quota` event. | `drain-resumed-quota` event after backoff or operator resume. |
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:424:| `paused-rate-limit` | Worker is sleeping out a rate-limit retry window inside an attempt. | Rate-limit response from harness. | Wait window elapses; worker resumes the same attempt. |
./docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md:425:| `exiting` | Worker is shutting down cleanly; will not claim more beads. | Operator stop or terminal failure. | Process exit. |

The worker list is populated from
     `GET /api/projects/:project/workers` (see FEAT-002). Each worker card
     renders the `current_attempt.phase` field as a color-coded phase badge:

     | Phase | Badge color |
     |-------|------------|
     | `queueing` | gray |
     | `launching` | blue |
     | `running` | green |
     | `post_checks` | yellow |
     | `landing` | purple |
     | `done` | teal |
     | `preserved` | orange |
     | `failed` | red |

   - Clicking a worker card expands to show the `recent_phases` timeline from
     `GET /api/projects/:project/workers/:id`. The expanded view also shows
     elapsed time, harness/model identity, cumulative token counts, and a link
     to the full execution evidence bundle for completed attempts.
   - For in-flight workers, the card auto-refreshes using the `workerProgress`
     subscription. The UI updates the phase badge, elapsed time, and token count from
     each progress event without re-fetching the full worker record. When the terminal
     phase event arrives, the stream closes and the card switches to a static completed state.

7. **Epic execution view**
   - Lists open epics separately from single-ticket ready work
   - Shows epic branch name, epic worktree path, active child bead, and merge
     gate status
   - Shows sequential child-bead history on the epic branch
   - Allows the user to inspect the final epic merge candidate and merge-gate
     results before the merge commit is created

8. **Provider / Harness Dashboard**

   A dedicated page — separate from the status dashboard, agent log monitor,
   and worker progress view — that gives operators a clear picture of
   configured providers and the routing signals DDx uses to make harness
   decisions.

   **Provider list panel (left/top):**
   - One row per configured harness (claude, codex, opencode, gemini, …)
   - Columns: harness name, display name, availability badge (`available` |
     `unavailable` | `unknown`), auth state badge, quota/headroom pill
     (`ok` | `blocked` | `unknown`), cost class, freshness timestamp
   - Searchable by harness name, model, status, or cost class
   - Sortable by any column; default sort by availability, then name
   - Filter chips: availability status, auth state, quota state, signal source
   - All data fetched from `GET /api/providers` on load; auto-refreshes on a
     configurable interval (default 60 s)

   **Provider detail panel (right/bottom, shown on row click):**
   - Full routing signal snapshot for the selected harness
   - Per-model quota/headroom with source attribution and `unknown` when no
     stable live source exists
   - Historical usage table: 7-day and 30-day windows for input/output/total
     tokens and cost; `unknown` rendered as `—` not `0`
   - Burn estimate row: daily token rate, subscription burn class, confidence
     label (`high` / `medium` / `low`), freshness timestamp
   - Performance signals: p50/p95 latency, success rate, sample count, window
   - Signal source provenance list: which sources contributed to this snapshot
     (e.g. `stats-cache`, `native-session-jsonl`, `ddx-metrics`, `none`)
   - Data fetched from `GET /api/providers/:harness` on panel open

   **Unknown-state semantics:**
   - `unknown` values are rendered with an explicit `—` or "unknown" label
     and a tooltip explaining why (e.g., "no stable non-PTY quota source
     confirmed for Claude")
   - Fields from provider-native sources are labeled "provider-reported"
   - Fields derived by DDx from observed metrics are labeled "DDx-estimated"
   - Fields that are genuinely absent carry a `?` badge, not a synthesized `0`

   **Relationship to other views:**
   - Provider dashboard is host+user global (not project-scoped); it is
     accessible from the top-level navigation bar alongside the project picker
   - The status dashboard worker cards link to worker detail and the agent log
     monitor — not to the provider dashboard
   - The agent log monitor session detail links to the provider dashboard for
     the harness used in that invocation (via harness name)
   - The provider dashboard does NOT embed worker progress, bead state, or
     execution history; those live in their own dedicated views

   **Reporting and export:**
   - "Copy JSON" action on the detail panel copies the `GET /api/providers/:harness`
     response verbatim — useful for bug reports and operator audits
   - Time-window selector (7d / 30d / custom) scopes the historical usage table

9. **Persona viewer**
   - Browse personas with descriptions and tags
   - See which roles each persona is bound to in project config
   - View full persona content rendered as markdown

### US-087: Operator Inspects Provider Availability and Routing Signals
**As an** operator about to queue a batch of agent work
**I want** to see configured providers with their availability, auth state,
  and quota/headroom at a glance
**So that** I can choose which harnesses are viable before dispatching

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given providers are configured, when I open the Provider Dashboard, then I
  see one row per harness with availability, auth state, and quota/headroom badges
- Given a provider's quota/headroom has no stable live source, then the badge
  shows "unknown" with a tooltip — not a fabricated `ok` or `blocked` value
- Given I click a row, then the detail panel opens showing the full routing
  signal snapshot with source attribution and freshness timestamp
- Given I click the harness link in the agent log monitor for an invocation,
  then I navigate to that harness's detail in the Provider Dashboard
- Given I navigate back from the provider detail, then I return to the provider
  list with the same sort and filter state

**E2E Test:** `providers.spec.ts` — full workflow (using fixture scenarios): open provider list → verify availability/auth/quota badges per fixture scenario → click row → verify detail panel → hover unknown badge → verify tooltip text → click harness link from agent log → verify navigation to provider detail → navigate back

### US-088: Operator Reviews Provider Utilization and Burn Rate
**As an** operator tracking subscription usage
**I want** to see historical token consumption, burn rate, and confidence level
  for each configured provider
**So that** I can balance load across providers and anticipate quota pressure

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given a provider has DDx-observed or provider-native usage history, when I
  view its detail panel, then I see a 7-day and 30-day usage table with
  input/output/total tokens and cost where known
- Given a token or cost field has no trustworthy source, then it renders as `—`
  not `0` or a fabricated value
- Given a burn estimate exists, then the detail panel shows the daily token rate,
  subscription burn class, confidence label, and the timestamp of the last
  contributing signal
- Given I want to share a provider's signal state with a colleague, then I can
  use "Copy JSON" to get the raw API response for the selected harness

**E2E Test:** `providers.spec.ts` — full workflow (using fixture scenarios): open provider detail → verify 7d/30d usage tables → verify `—` for missing values → verify burn estimate row → click Copy JSON → verify clipboard contents match API response → change time window → verify table updates

### US-088b: Operator Distinguishes Source Types in Provider Detail
**As an** operator debugging a routing decision
**I want** to know whether each signal field came from the provider directly,
  DDx estimation, or is unknown
**So that** I can trust or question the data DDx is routing on

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given a field came from a provider-native source (e.g. `stats-cache`), then
  a "provider-reported" label or badge is shown
- Given a field was derived by DDx from observed invocation metrics, then a
  "DDx-estimated" label is shown
- Given no source exists for a field, then a `?` badge appears — no `0` or
  inferred value is displayed

	import { gql } from 'graphql-request';
	import FilterChip from '$lib/components/FilterChip.svelte';

	const AGENT_METRICS_QUERY = gql`
		query AgentMetricsByProvider($window: AgentMetricsWindow!) {
			agentMetrics(window: $window, groupBy: PROVIDER) {
				window
				groupBy
				revision
				rows {
					key
					attempts
					successes
					successRate
					meanDurationMs
					p50DurationMs
					p95DurationMs
					meanCostUsd
					effectiveCostPerSuccessUsd
					meanInputTokens
					meanOutputTokens
					lastSeenAt
				}
			}
		}
	`;

	interface AgentMetricsRow {
		key: string;
		attempts: number;
		successes: number;
		successRate: number;
		meanDurationMs: number;
		p50DurationMs: number;
		p95DurationMs: number;
		meanCostUsd: number;
		effectiveCostPerSuccessUsd: number | null;
		meanInputTokens: number;
		meanOutputTokens: number;
		lastSeenAt: string | null;
	}

	type SectionKey = 'availability' | 'performance';
	type WindowKey = 'W24H' | 'W7D' | 'W30D';
	const WINDOW_LABEL: Record<WindowKey, string> = { W24H: '24h', W7D: '7d', W30D: '30d' };

	const PROVIDER_STATUSES_QUERY = gql`
		query ProviderStatuses {
			providerStatuses {
				name
				kind
				providerType
				baseURL
				model
				status
				reachable
				detail
				modelCount
				isDefault
				cooldownUntil
				lastCheckedAt
				defaultForProfile
				recentWorkerCount
				usage {
					tokensUsedLastHour
					tokensUsedLast24h
					requestsLastHour
					requestsLast24h
				}
				quota {
					ceilingTokens
					ceilingWindowSeconds
					remaining
					resetAt
				}
				sparkline
			}
			harnessStatuses {
				name
				kind
				providerType
				baseURL
				model
				status
				reachable
				detail
				modelCount
				isDefault
				cooldownUntil
				lastCheckedAt
				defaultForProfile
				recentWorkerCount
				usage {
					tokensUsedLastHour
					tokensUsedLast24h
					requestsLastHour
					requestsLast24h
				}
				quota {
					ceilingTokens
					ceilingWindowSeconds
					remaining
					resetAt
				}
				sparkline
			}
		}
	`;

	const PROVIDER_MODELS_QUERY = gql`
		query ProviderModels($name: String!, $kind: ProviderKind!) {
			providerModels(name: $name, kind: $kind) {
				name
				kind
				baseURL
				fetchedAt
				fromCache
				models {
					id
					contextLength
					available
				}
			}
		}
	`;

	const REFRESH_PROVIDER_MODELS_MUTATION = gql`
		mutation RefreshProviderModels($name: String!, $kind: ProviderKind!) {
			refreshProviderModels(name: $name, kind: $kind) {
				name
				kind
				baseURL
				fetchedAt
				fromCache
				models {
					id
					contextLength
					available
				}
			}
		}
	`;

	const DEFAULT_ROUTE_QUERY = gql`
		query DefaultRouteStatus {
			defaultRouteStatus {
				modelRef
				resolvedProvider
				resolvedModel
				strategy
			}
		}
	`;

	interface ProviderUsage {
		tokensUsedLastHour: number | null;
		tokensUsedLast24h: number | null;
		requestsLastHour: number | null;
		requestsLast24h: number | null;
	}

	interface ProviderQuota {
		ceilingTokens: number | null;
		ceilingWindowSeconds: number | null;
		remaining: number | null;
		resetAt: string | null;
	}

	interface ProviderStatus {
		name: string;
		kind: 'ENDPOINT' | 'HARNESS';
		providerType: string;
		baseURL: string;
		model: string;
		status: string;
		reachable: boolean;
		detail: string;
		modelCount: number;
		isDefault: boolean;
		cooldownUntil: string | null;
		lastCheckedAt: string | null;
		defaultForProfile: string[];
		recentWorkerCount: number;
		usage: ProviderUsage | null;
		quota: ProviderQuota | null;
		sparkline: number[];
	}

	interface ProviderModelEntry {
		id: string;
		contextLength: number | null;
		available: boolean;
	}

	interface ProviderModelsResult {
		name: string;
		kind: 'ENDPOINT' | 'HARNESS';
		baseURL: string;
		fetchedAt: string;
		fromCache: boolean;
		models: ProviderModelEntry[];
	}

	interface DefaultRouteStatus {
		modelRef: string;
		resolvedProvider: string | null;
		resolvedModel: string | null;
		strategy: string | null;
	}

	// First-paint state: we render the table from the query result as soon as
	// it lands. The query itself returns cached probe results — a live refresh
	// action will enqueue fresh probes in a future iteration.
	let rows = $state<ProviderStatus[]>([]);
	let defaultRoute = $state<DefaultRouteStatus | null>(null);

data.text: The list view already exposes a provider hyperlink, so the likely gap is proof and consistency, not the link helper itself. I’m checking the worker detail page next because that’s the other obvious place where the provider identity might need to be clickable, and I want to avoid duplicating a link that already exists elsewhere.

data.text: I’ve confirmed the gap: the live worker table already links the provider, but the reported-worker rows only show plain harness text. I’m wiring that column to the provider detail page and adding a navigation test so the worker view now proves the cross-link end to end.
The new navigation assertion needs a reported-worker fixture, so I’m extending the worker e2e mock to serve that data explicitly instead of relying on whatever the live server happens to return.

The implementation change is in place. I’m switching to verification now: first the targeted workers/providers Playwright tests, then the repo’s pre-commit gate so I can catch formatting or lint fallout before I commit.

data.text: The navigation test failed for a simple reason: the fixture node ID is not the hard-coded `node-abc` stub in the test file. I’m switching the assertion to the live node ID from the fixture so the link check matches the actual URL the harness serves.

data.text: The last failure exposed a matching bug in the GraphQL mock: the reported-workers query name contains `WorkersByProject`, so it was being swallowed by the live-workers branch. I’m moving the reported-workers handler ahead of that broader match so the panel actually receives the fixture row.

data.text:
Implemented and committed as `1da49c43` (`fix: link workers to provider detail [ddx-99419bc1]`).

- The reported-workers rows on the Workers tab now link the harness name to the provider detail page via `providerHref()` in [`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte`](.../cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/workers/+layout.svelte#L568).
- Added e2e coverage in [`cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/workers.spec.ts`](.../cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/workers.spec.ts#L153) that:
  - verifies the reported-worker provider link href
  - clicks a live worker provider link and asserts navigation to `/nodes/<nodeId>/providers/claude`
- Provider rows already expose `recentWorkerCount`; that behavior is covered in [`cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/providers.spec.ts`](.../cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/providers.spec.ts#L142).

Verification:
- `bun run check`
- `bun run test:e2e -- e2e/providers.spec.ts`
- `bun run test:e2e -- e2e/workers.spec.ts`
- `lefthook run pre-commit`
